text: The right colon is fully mobilized, and the mesentery is dissected up to the planned transection sites, showing clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to assess the perfusion of the tissue at the planned anastomosis site, ensuring adequate blood flow for successful healing.
The surgeon places an anti-mesenteric suture on both the colon and the small bowel, preparing these structures for the creation of the anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares for staple placement using a twelve millimeter port in the right lower quadrant, indicating that the technique can be performed laparoscopically or robotically.
The surgeon cuts the suture to a length of twenty centimeters, preparing to use it for two separate stay sutures. The first suture is placed, followed by another at eight centimeters distally.
The surgeon uses the additional arm of the robot to hold the small bowel in place, while a short suture is applied for orientation purposes rather than strength.

The surgeon maneuvers underneath the mesentery, revealing a wide-open defect for the stapler passage. The colon is opened slightly medial toward the mesenteric side to stabilize it for stapler insertion. A small portion of the remaining ligament of Treitz is removed.
The surgeon opens the small bowel on the antimesenteric side, with suction available if necessary.
The surgeon positions the stapler with its thin side towards the small bowel and the larger side towards the colon, ensuring proper alignment to facilitate smooth insertion and minimize twisting.

The surgeon holds the stay suture, allowing the mesentery to fall away, and inserts the stapler fully to achieve a wide anastomosis. The stapler is lifted anteriorly towards the abdominal wall to ensure complete separation of the mesentery.
The surgeon closes the stapler and uses it for orientation before firing it, ensuring proper alignment between the small bowel and colon for the anastomosis.
The surgeon reaches into the surgical field to grasp the back wall of the common enterotomy and colotomy, ensuring proper positioning for the second stapler to fully remove the specimen side tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates the perfusion up to the planned transection site, showing a clear distinction between the perfused tissue and the lack of perfusion on the specimen side, distal to the proximal stay suture.

The surgeon uses the distal stay suture to orient the specimen, ensuring a perpendicular staple across the original staple line. Despite the excellent mobilization of the tissue, two staple firings are required to complete the transection.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the mesenteric tissue, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The stapler is positioned to make a perpendicular cut for the second firing.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a large thoracic endo catch bag through a fifteen millimeter port at the extraction site.
